reasoning: The existence of a genuine circuit split (3rd Circuit vs 9th/11th Circuits) regarding FIFRA preemption of state-law failure-to-warn claims significantly elevates the likelihood of the Supreme Court granting certiorari. Monsanto's petition in Durnell, filed on April 4, 2025, aligns perfectly with the Court's schedule to be considered during the 'Long Conference' in late September 2025. If the Court simply grants the petition, it will comfortably happen before the January 15, 2026 deadline. However, three factors substantially suppress this probability. First, Durnell is a state appellate court case, which historically carries a higher likelihood of 'vehicle problems' (e.g., independent state law grounds or preservation issues) compared to federal appeals. Second, because of the new Trump administration and the flip-flopping history of the Solicitor General's stance on this exact issue (having previously issued a CVSG in Hardeman), the Court has a high chance of requesting the views of the SG (CVSG). A CVSG in October 2025 would almost certainly delay the cert decision until after January 2026. Third, the Court could decide to grant a different pending case on the same issue and hold Durnell, which would result in a prolonged hold extending far beyond January 2026. Combining an elevated base rate for a recognized circuit split with the timeline strictures and potential vehicle issues, an estimate of 33% balances these structural barriers against the clear appellate division.
